gold_final_answer: All functions f(x) = ax + b with a ≠ 0.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Injectivity / surjectivity
response_text:
Let  
\[
P(x,y,z):\quad f(x),f(y),f(z)\text{ are in arithmetic progression } \Longrightarrow x,y,z\text{ are in arithmetic progression.}
\]
Condition (ii) says precisely that for all \(a,b,c\in\mathbb R\),
\[
2f(b)=f(a)+f(c)\implies 2b=a+c.
\]

We seek all continuous surjective \(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) satisfying this and
\[
2f(1)=f(0)+f(2). \tag{1}
\]

We will show that the only such functions are
\[
f(x)=x+\beta \quad\text{or}\quad f(x)=-x+\beta
\]
for some real constant \(\beta\).

---

## 1. First consequences of (ii)

We first show \(f\) is injective.

If \(f(x)=f(y)\), then \(f(x),f(x),f(y)\) are in arithmetic progression, so by (ii), \(x,x,y\) are in arithmetic progression:
\[
2x=x+y \implies x=y.
\]
Hence \(f\) is injective.

Since \(f\) is continuous and injective on \(\mathbb R\), it is strictly monotone. Since it is also surjective onto \(\mathbb R\), it is a homeomorphism of \(\mathbb R\), so \(f^{-1}\) exists and is continuous.

Now rewrite (ii) using injectivity. If
\[
2f(b)=f(a)+f(c),
\]
then \(2b=a+c\). Equivalently, if three values of \(f\) are in arithmetic progression, then the corresponding arguments are too.

Because \(f\) is surjective, given any \(u,v,w\in\mathbb R\) with \(2v=u+w\), we may write
\[
u=f(a),\quad v=f(b),\quad w=f(c)
\]
for some \(a,b,c\). Then \(f(a),f(b),f(c)\) are in arithmetic progression, so \(2b=a+c\). Therefore
\[
2f^{-1}(v)=f^{-1}(u)+f^{-1}(w).
\]
So \(f^{-1}\) also preserves arithmetic progressions.

Thus both \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\) send arithmetic progressions to arithmetic progressions.

---

## 2. Midpoint preservation

Take any \(x,y\in\mathbb R\), and set
\[
m=\frac{x+y}{2}.
\]
Then \(x,m,y\) are in arithmetic progression, so \(f(x),f(m),f(y)\) are also in arithmetic progression. Why? Since \(f^{-1}\) has property (ii), applying it to the triple \(f(x),f(m),f(y)\) gives the reverse implication; equivalently, because \(x,m,y\) are AP and \(f\) is bijective with AP-reflecting inverse, \(f\) must map APs to APs as well.

Hence
\[
2f\!\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=f(x)+f(y). \tag{2}
\]
So \(f\) preserves midpoints.

---

## 3. Jensen equation and linearity

A continuous function satisfying the midpoint identity (2) on \(\mathbb R\) is affine. Briefly:

Let
\[
g(x)=f(x)-f(0).
\]
Then \(g\) is continuous and
\[
g\!\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\frac{g(x)+g(y)}{2}.
\]
By standard dyadic induction,
\[
g\big((1-t)x+ty\big)=(1-t)g(x)+tg(y)
\]
for all dyadic rational \(t\in[0,1]\), and by continuity for all real \(t\in[0,1]\). Taking \(y=0\), we get
\[
g(tx)=tg(x)\qquad (t\in[0,1]).
\]
Then extending to all real \(t\) gives additivity and homogeneity, so
\[
f(x)=\alpha x+\beta
\]
for some real constants \(\alpha,\beta\).

Since \(f\) is surjective, \(\alpha\neq 0\).

---

## 4. Use condition (ii)

Now let
\[
f(x)=\alpha x+\beta,\qquad \alpha\neq 0.
\]
Suppose \(f(a),f(b),f(c)\) are in arithmetic progression. Then
\[
2(\alpha b+\beta)=(\alpha a+\beta)+(\alpha c+\beta)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
2\alpha b=\alpha a+\alpha c.
\]
Since \(\alpha\neq 0\),
\[
2b=a+c,
\]
so (ii) holds automatically for every nonzero \(\alpha\).

Thus all continuous surjective affine functions satisfy (ii).

---

## 5. Use condition (i)

Condition (i):
\[
2f(1)=f(0)+f(2).
\]
For \(f(x)=\alpha x+\beta\),
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)=\beta+(2\alpha+\beta),
\]
which is an identity. So (i) imposes no extra restriction beyond affinity.

But we still need the implication in the reverse direction used above. For affine \(f(x)=\alpha x+\beta\), if \(a,b,c\) are in arithmetic progression then clearly \(f(a),f(b),f(c)\) are also in arithmetic progression. Since \(f^{-1}\) also has the same form, our earlier reasoning is consistent.

Finally, because \(f\) is injective and strictly monotone, and both \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\) preserve arithmetic progressions, the only possible slopes are actually \(\pm1\)? No: condition (ii) alone allows any nonzero \(\alpha\). But the problem also gave only
\[
2f(1)=f(0)+f(2),
\]
which every affine function satisfies. So we must check carefully whether all nonzero \(\alpha\) work.

Indeed, for \(f(x)=2x\),
if \(f(a),f(b),f(c)\) are in AP, then \(2b=a+c\); surjective? No, \(2x\) is surjective onto \(\mathbb R\). So it works.

Therefore all affine functions with nonzero slope satisfy both conditions.

---

Final answer: \(\boxed{f(x)=\alpha x+\beta\ \text{ for arbitrary real }\beta\text{ and arbitrary nonzero real }\alpha}\).
